terms.definition: A region of a file (or a file-level disagreement) that Git's three-way merge cannot reconcile automatically; requires human resolution.
Merge algorithm that uses the common ancestor of two branches plus their two tips to decide which side each change came from.
The `<<<<<<<` / `=======` / `>>>>>>>` (and optional `||||||| ancestor`) block Git writes into a file when it can't decide.
The most recent commit reachable from both branches; the baseline against which both sides' changes are computed.
Conflict-marker style that includes the common ancestor between the two diverging sides; enabled with `git config merge.conflictStyle diff3`.
A logical inconsistency between two branches (e.g. one renames a function, the other adds a caller) that Git auto-merges into broken code; only tests catch these.
Wrapper that opens a configured 3-way diff editor (VS Code, kdiff3, meld, vimdiff) on each conflicted file.
`reuse recorded resolution` — Git's optional feature that remembers your conflict resolutions and applies them automatically to identical conflicts later.
